items.correct: The square root of x (the positive root when x is positive).
items.misconception: x^(1/2) means x divided by 2 — half of x.
items.random_wrong: x^(1/2) means 1 divided by x squared.
items.why: Fraction-as-division intuition takes over; students don't connect fractional exponents to roots.

items.misconception: (x + 3)^2 equals x^2 + 9 because squaring distributes over addition.
items.why: Students apply the (false) distributive rule (a + b)^n = a^n + b^n, missing the cross term from the actual expansion.

items.q: If a data set has an extreme outlier, which average is most affected?
items.correct: The mean — because every value (including the outlier) contributes to it.
items.misconception: The median is most affected because the outlier shifts the middle.
items.random_wrong: The mode is most affected because the outlier becomes the new most common value.
items.why: Students don't distinguish how each average is computed; they think any extreme value disturbs all measures equally.
Correct answer: gcd. “lcm” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The real figure is 7. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
